metadata.title: PC Algorithm: Learning Causal Structure from Data

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The idea that missing edges correspond to conditional independencies, and that PC only orients v-structures uniquely because Markov-equivalent graphs exist, finally clicked — especially the false X1-X4 edge that appeared then got pruned. And the 500-observations-per-edge rule of thumb is something I want to test. Walk me through: (1) building a skeleton via partial-correlation tests and orienting v-structures on a known 5-variable DAG; (2) sweeping n from 200 to 5000 and plotting skeleton precision and recall; (3) marking the n where recovery becomes reliable and relating it to my graph's edge count.

props.command: The idea that missing edges correspond to conditional independencies, and that PC only orients v-structures uniquely because Markov-equivalent graphs exist, finally clicked — especially the false X1-X4 edge that appeared then got pruned. And the 500-observations-per-edge rule of thumb is something I want to test. Walk me through: (1) building a skeleton via partial-correlation tests and orienting v-structures on a known 5-variable DAG; (2) sweeping n from 200 to 5000 and plotting skeleton precision and recall; (3) marking the n where recovery becomes reliable and relating it to my graph's edge count.
props.segment: PC Algorithm: Learning Causal Structure from

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the stakes — BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: You have 5 variables and no theory about which causes which. Can the PC algorithm learn the causal graph from conditional independence tests alone?
beats.visual_intent: title card: 'causal discovery — learning the graph from data'; no domain knowledge assumed

beats.narration_text: Ask Claude. Implement a simplified PC algorithm: build a skeleton via partial correlation tests, orient v-structures, compare to ground truth DAG. Print precision, recall, and structural Hamming distance.

props.command: claude "Implement simplified PC algorithm for causal discovery. Ground truth DAG: X1->X2, X1->X3, X2->X4, X3->X4, X4->X5, X2->X5. Generate n=2000 from linear Gaussian SCM. Implement: skeleton via partial correlation tests (p<0.05), v-structure orientation. Compare to ground truth: print precision, recall, structural Hamming distance."

beats.narration_text: Read it. The PC algorithm exploits the Markov condition: conditional independencies in data correspond to missing edges in the DAG. It cannot orient all edges — Markov equivalent graphs exist — but it orients v-structures uniquely.

The animated DAG construction shows the algorithm's steps. The skeleton appears as undirected gray lines. A false positive edge between X1 and X4 appears then disappears when pruned. Then the green arrows orient the true structure. Precision 0.86, recall 1.0, SHD 1.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: animated DAG construction with skeleton, pruning, and orientation steps

beats.narration_text: Now ask Claude to run the PC algorithm at n=200, n=500, n=2000, and n=5000. Show how precision and recall improve with sample size — at what n does the algorithm reliably recover the true skeleton?

props.command:
claude "update pc_algorithm.py:
  -> run at n=200, 500, 2000, 5000 (10 random seeds each)
  -> print mean precision, recall, SHD at each n
  -> identify minimum n for skeleton precision > 0.8"

beats.narration_text: At n=200, skeleton precision is 0.61 — noisy. At n=500, it crosses 0.75. At n=2000, it reaches 0.86. The PC algorithm needs roughly 500 observations per edge to achieve reliable recovery — a useful rule of thumb for power analysis.
beats.visual_intent: slate: precision/recall/SHD vs n table; convergence curve

beats.narration_text: The PC algorithm trades domain knowledge for data. It learns what domain knowledge usually asserts for free. The cost: it needs data, it struggles with latent variables, and Markov equivalent graphs cannot be distinguished without more assumptions.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: PC algorithm tradeoffs; Markov equivalence class limitation

beats.narration_text: Your move: draw the DAG you currently assume for your domain and run the PC algorithm on your data. Where the algorithm's skeleton contradicts your assumed graph, you have a disagreement between domain knowledge and statistical independence. Investigate both — one of them is wrong.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: run PC algorithm, compare to assumed DAG, investigate disagreements

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. The PC algorithm trades domain knowledge for data. It learns what domain knowledge usually asserts for free. The cost: it needs data, it struggles with latent variables, and Markov equivalent graphs cannot be distinguished without more assumptions. Your move: draw the DAG you currently assume for your domain and run the PC algorithm on your data. Where the algorithm's skeleton contradicts your assumed graph, you have a disagreement between domain knowledge and statistical independence. Investigate both — one of them is wrong.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [PC Algorithm: Learning Causal Structure from Data] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
